parseland_candidate.abstract:
Selected foliar broadcast-applied insecticides were evaluated for control of thrips in cotton. ‘FM 1944 GLB2’ cotton was planted 26 Apr at the Virginia Tech Tidewater Agric. Res. & Ext. Ctr., Suffolk, VA, using 36-inch row spacing. Aeris-treated seed was used for all plots. Single foliar applications of Radiant, Orthene, Exirel, and Minecto Pro were made at the first true leaf stage on 19 May with a CO2-pressurized backpack sprayer. The broadcast boom was calibrated to deliver 14.3 gpa at 18 psi through 8002EVS nozzles spaced 18 inches apart. A randomized complete block experimental design was used with four replicates; plots were four rows by 35 ft. Thrips injury to plants was determined on three dates by visually rating injury using a 0–5 scale, where 0 = no thrips induced plant injury; 1 = 10% injured leaves, no bud injury; 2 = 25% injured leaves, no bud injury; 3 = 75% injured leaves, and 0–25% buds injured; 4 = 90% injured leaves, and >25% buds injured; and 5 = dead plants. Thrips counts from check plots were taken on three dates by collecting five plants per plot in Mason jars containing ∼500 ml soapy water. Immature and adult thrips were counted under a stereoscope following suction filtration. Yield was determined by harvesting two rows of each plot using a commercial 2-row cotton picker. Sub-samples were ginned to determine lint versus seed and trash weight (42.2% lint, 57.8% seed and trash). Data were analyzed using ANOVA and LSD statistical procedures.
There were significant differences in thrips injury ratings between treatments on all sample dates, with all insecticides resulting in less injury relative to the untreated check (Table 1). Thrips populations per five plants in the untreated check plots peaked at eight adults at the first true leaf stage on 24 May, and 16 immatures at the fourth true leaf stage on 6 Jun (data not shown). There were no significant differences in yield among treatments. 
Means within a column followed by the same letter(s) are not significantly different (LSD, P = 0.05).
Thrips injury rated on a 0–5 scale, 0 = no thrips injury and 5 = dead plants.
Means within a column followed by the same letter(s) are not significantly different (LSD, P = 0.05).
Thrips injury rated on a 0–5 scale, 0 = no thrips injury and 5 = dead plants.
Section Editor: Donald Cook

parseland_candidate.abstract: This chapter discusses the ‘fog of peace’ that affects the negotiation on agrarian issues that is at the heart of the ongoing Colombian peace process. The ‘fog of peace’ takes place because the politicians who can make peace must have resources to speak to both state challengers and vested interests. But the very skills and networks that allow them to initiate the process may hinder their capacity, or willingness, to implement consistent pro-peace policies and coalitions. Furthermore, they operate in turbulent and uncertain settings, in which they have to juggle several contradictory interests. In this kind of context, decision-makers face complex sequencing problems, e.g. whether to promote policies that foster long-term stability at the risk of triggering short-term contention, or let the policies die away but then leave intact the very structures that might have contributed to the onset and/or persistence of the conflict in the first place.

parseland_candidate.abstract: Up to the present, most computer graphics consoles have been connected directly to dedicated general purpose machines. These graphics systems have required from the user a large entry fee. Much of the work which has been done on them can now be done on less sophisticated equipment, some of which is described in this paper.

evidence_excerpt:
"ArticleFulltext">

Abstract

Up to the present, most computer graphics consoles have been connected directly to dedicated general purpose machines. These graphics systems have required from the user a large entry fee. Much of the work which has been done on them can now be done on less sophisticated equipment, some of which is described in this paper.

parseland_candidate.abstract:
The raft of military coups d'état in Latin America against quasi-democratic governments in the 1960s and 1970s was initially dubbed the ‘new authoritarianism’; this was notwithstanding the pre-emptive reformist coup in Peru in 1968, which was deemed an outlier. More recently, Kurt Weyland broadly interpreted these events as a prolonged ‘backlash’ to the regional political effervescence in the wake of the Cuban Revolution, while Hal Brands advanced a ‘theory of the two demons’, whereby left-wing violence simply begot brutal counter-revolution (see Weyland's Revolution and reaction, Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2019 and Brands's Latin America's Cold War, Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 2012).
This edited volume brings together contributions from notable specialists in the field to provide a more nuanced analysis of political retrogression throughout the region. The focus is not just on the usual suspects—Brazil (1964), Uruguay (1973), Chile (1973) and Argentina (1976). The coup in Peru is not treated as an anomaly; it is examined alongside the understudied 1972 reformist coup in Honduras and the first iteration of the Junta Revolucionaria de Gobierno (JRG) in El Salvador in October 1979. Surprisingly, the similarly reformist regimes in Ecuador (1972–5) and Panama (1968–81) inexplicably only warrant a passing mention even though they conform at least pallidly to the Peruvian paradigm (p. 123). The book is also noteworthy for extending its gaze beyond South America.

parseland_candidate.abstract: Chapter 30 of Within You Without You, “You’ve Got to Leave to Come Back,” closely examines George Harrison’s “comeback” album of late 1987, Cloud Nine. Powered by a cover version of an old, obscure R&B number, “Got My Mind Set on You,” which made Harrison the last ex-Beatle to have a number-one hit, the album reintroduced Harrison to a whole new generation, given the heavy rotation his singles received on MTV. It reminded old fans that he was still alive and a creative force. The album paired Harrison with Jeff Lynne, the founder of Electric Light Orchestra (ELO) and a huge Beatles fan who was shifting his attention away from performing in favor of being an album producer. The album also set the stage for several future collaborations with Lynne, including the formation of the Traveling Wilburys supergroup and the mid-1990s archival Beatles project, Anthology.
